title: In Marge We Trust

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Opening sequence car crash
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature exclamation after mishap
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer catchphrase, perfectly integrated with his character but routine by this point

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: but I got a lot of work to do around the bed
jokes.setup: Homer making excuse to avoid church
jokes.punchline: Absurd notion that lying in bed constitutes 'work'
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific humor showing Homer's lazy rationalization

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I can't go to church, Marge. I'm working.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're lying in bed.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly. It's hard work.

jokes.text: In that case, he should've made the week an hour longer. Lousy God.
jokes.setup: Marge saying God only asks for an hour a week
jokes.punchline: Homer's backwards logic and casual blasphemy
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist logic with Homer's characteristic irreverence toward authority figures

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: God only asks for an hour a week.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's it? In that case, I'm willing to give it a shot.

jokes.text: [Yawns] Damn it!
jokes.setup: Minister giving boring sermon about constancy
jokes.punchline: Accidentally swearing in church, then losing his place
jokes.explanation: Good irony of minister swearing during sermon, with appropriate consequences

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: And so we see that constancy is a virtue that—oh, for the love of—

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Now where was I?

jokes.text: [Moe] Aw, for the love of crumb cake.
jokes.setup: Lovejoy starting sermon over again
jokes.punchline: Moe's oddball euphemism instead of standard curse
jokes.explanation: Characteristically odd Moe expression, creative alternative to standard profanity

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: And that's why we must all seek forgiveness for our sins.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Oh, for the love of—! What the—?! Are you frick-frackin' kidding me?!

jokes.text: [Bird Screeching] [All Gasping]
jokes.setup: Boring sermon droning on with people falling asleep
jokes.punchline: Bird crashes through window, congregation suddenly alert
jokes.explanation: Good visual contrast between sleepy congregation and sudden alertness

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: And so, as I was saying, the path to righteousness is long and winding, much like the road to... to...

dialogue.speaker: Congregation
dialogue.line: [snoring, sleeping]

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: ...Springfield, and the Lord has blessed us with...

dialogue.speaker: Congregation
dialogue.line: [continues sleeping]
[Bird crashes through window]

dialogue.speaker: Congregation
dialogue.line: [suddenly awake and alert, gasping]

jokes.text: It's the longest possible time before more church.
jokes.setup: Bart saying leaving church is the best part of the week
jokes.punchline: Mathematical precision applied to avoiding church
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and child's logic perfectly applied to universal experience

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Leaving church is the best part of the week.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What about donuts?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, okay, donuts are number one. But leaving church is a very close number two.

jokes.text: It's starting to turn brown.
jokes.setup: Getting rid of Christmas tree in what appears to be spring
jokes.punchline: Massive understatement about obviously dead tree
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan delivery of obvious understatement about procrastination

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I guess we should get rid of this Christmas tree.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, it's April.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's looking a little brown.

jokes.text: Can you believe it? They give you five Q's and only two U's. What a world.
jokes.setup: Helen playing word game with Marge
jokes.punchline: Treating Scrabble tile distribution as cosmic injustice
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Helen's tendency toward melodrama over trivial things

dialogue.speaker: Helen Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Oh my stars! I got four U's and no vowels that matter!

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: Helen, U is a vowel.

dialogue.speaker: Helen Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Well, I never! The universe is against me!

jokes.text: I wasn't prepared for that.
jokes.setup: Helen challenging Marge to volunteer, Marge immediately agreeing
jokes.punchline: Helen's honest admission she expected resistance
jokes.explanation: Good honest reaction beat showing Helen's manipulative expectations backfiring

dialogue.speaker: Helen Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Well, I'm so glad you volunteered, Marge. I must say, I fully expected you to put up more of a fight.

dialogue.speaker: Marge Simpson
dialogue.line: Oh, well, I'm always happy to help out.

dialogue.speaker: Helen Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that's the problem. It takes all the fun out of it.

jokes.text: Hey, here's a perfectly good basketball half.
jokes.setup: Family scrounging at the dump
jokes.punchline: Homer's optimistic assessment of clearly useless trash
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist Homer logic finding value in obviously worthless items

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look at this! A perfectly good TV with only a few bullet holes in it.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, it's completely destroyed.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Nonsense! I just need to find the right angle to watch it from.

jokes.text: I found a Malibu Stacy with no head.
jokes.setup: Children finding treasures at dump
jokes.punchline: Lisa's matter-of-fact description of mutilated doll
jokes.explanation: Mild dark humor in child's casual description of disturbing toy

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Look, a doll! Oh, her head's been chewed off. Well, at least she's dead, so she can't feel the pain.

jokes.text: [Bart] Maybe it's a box from the future! [Lisa] It looks Japanese.
jokes.setup: Discovering mysterious box
jokes.punchline: Bart's wild speculation vs Lisa's practical observation
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast between Bart's imagination and Lisa's logic

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa! Check it out, Lisa! Some kind of mysterious box!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It could be anything! Maybe it's full of treasure, or aliens, or a portal to another dimension!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, it's just a cardboard box. There's a shipping label from an office supply store.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's exactly what they want us to think!

jokes.text: Wh-Why am I on a Japanese box?
jokes.setup: Homer discovering his face on foreign product
jokes.punchline: Homer's confusion at impossible coincidence
jokes.explanation: Good setup for episode's central absurd premise - Homer's face on random Japanese product

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. My face is on a Brazilian soda bottle? How is that possible?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I've never even been to Brazil... have I?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, who am I kidding? Of course I haven't been to Brazil.

jokes.text: Do you know, thanks to you, I've rediscovered a form of shame that's gone unused for 700 years?
jokes.setup: Lovejoy praising Marge's church help
jokes.punchline: Overly specific historical reference to personal embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Creative absurdist line with specific historical pretension typical of Simpsons writing

jokes.text: We rented Man Without a Face. I didn't even know he had a problem!
jokes.setup: Skinner calling for relationship advice
jokes.punchline: Literal interpretation of movie title showing his naivety
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-specific humor showing Skinner's literal-mindedness and sheltered life

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: I need relationship advice. My girlfriend left me because I'm too literal-minded.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: That's rough. Have you tried watching some romantic movies?

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: I watched 'Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind.' Now I'm trying to find a neurologist to erase my memories.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: That's not what it means, man.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, it says 'eternal sunshine' right there. I've been standing outside for three hours waiting for the sun to never set.

jokes.text: Oh, it's all good.
jokes.setup: Skinner asking for specific Biblical passage
jokes.punchline: Lovejoy's lazy, unhelpful response
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lovejoy's apathy toward his pastoral duties

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: Reverend, I need to find a specific Biblical passage. Can you help me locate it?

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Oh, well, you know, there's so many of those passages. Have you tried just... not worrying about it?

jokes.text: The '60s were long over, and people were once again ready to feel bad about themselves.
jokes.setup: Lovejoy's flashback to idealistic youth
jokes.punchline: Cynical observation about cyclical cultural guilt
jokes.explanation: Sharp cultural observation with dark wit about American psychological cycles

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: I used to believe I could change the world with my faith and compassion.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Now I realize people just want absolution without actually changing anything. They'll feel guilty, confess, get forgiven, then do it all again next week.

jokes.text: Well, I was talked into doing a dance called the bump, but my hip slipped, and my- my buttocks came into contact with the buttocks of another young man!
jokes.setup: Young Ned confessing to young Lovejoy
jokes.punchline: Overly clinical description of innocent dance move as moral crisis
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned Flanders character moment - clinical language for innocent situation treated as moral crisis

dialogue.speaker: Young Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Reverend Lovejoy, I've committed a terrible sin. I was dancing with a girl, and... and our hips were touching.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Ned, that's not a sin. That's just dancing.

dialogue.speaker: Young Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: But the friction between our pelvises generated an impure thought, and I experienced a involuntary tumescence of the genital area!

jokes.text: Well, l- I think I may be coveting my own wife.
jokes.setup: Series of neurotic Ned confessions
jokes.punchline: Ned worried about biblically inappropriate feelings toward his own spouse
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic showing Ned's religious neurosis taken to extreme - worrying about coveting his own wife

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: I've been having the most untoward thoughts about Maude.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Feelings of a... conjugal nature.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Is it sinful to desire one's own wife in such a carnal way?

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: I consulted the Good Book, but it doesn't specifically address such wickedness between husband and wife.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Okily-dokily, I'm going straight to Hell.

jokes.text: Luckily, by then it was the '80s, and no one noticed.
jokes.setup: Lovejoy explaining how he stopped caring about parishioners
jokes.punchline: Cynical observation about 1980s cultural self-absorption
jokes.explanation: Sharp cultural satire about 1980s materialism and narcissism enabling pastoral negligence

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: I used to care about my parishioners, but then I realized something in the 1980s: nobody cares about anybody. It's all about me, me, me. So now I just say whatever gets the biggest laugh and move on to the next sermon.

jokes.text: Oh, well, this is, um, the, uh- the Listen Lady.
jokes.setup: Marge answering church phone unprepared
jokes.punchline: Improvised superhero-style alter ego name
jokes.explanation: Good setup for episode subplot - Marge's accidental counseling career with made-up title

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Hello, St. Sebastian's Church... Oh, you need help? Well, I'm just a volunteer, but... I suppose I could try. From this moment on, call me... Captain Marge!

jokes.text: All righty. Uh, number one, I've lost the will to live.
jokes.setup: Moe calling for help with problems
jokes.punchline: Casually listing existential crisis as first item
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe character moment - treating suicidal depression as mundane list item

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: I'm having an existential crisis, my bar's losing money, and I think my cat's plotting against me.

jokes.text: Oh, that's ridiculous, Moe. You've got lots to live for.
jokes.setup: Moe's anonymous cry for help
jokes.punchline: Marge immediately recognizing caller and dismissing his despair
jokes.explanation: Good character interaction - Marge's maternal instincts overriding anonymity

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Hello, is this the suicide hotline?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Yes, it is. Who am I speaking with?

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: This is... uh... just some guy. You don't know me.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Moe, is that you?

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: No! I told ya, you don't know me!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Moe, I recognize your voice. Now stop being silly and go home.

jokes.text: Do you know that so-called volunteers don't even get paid?
jokes.setup: Homer criticizing Marge's volunteering
jokes.punchline: Completely missing the point of volunteerism
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - fundamentally misunderstanding concept of volunteering

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, you've been volunteering at the library for three weeks and we haven't gotten a single free book out of it.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's not how volunteering works. It's about giving back to the community.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Giving back? I've been giving my whole life, Marge. My sweat, my tears, my occasional blood at the plasma donation center. When do I get something back?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: The point is to help others without expecting a reward.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, that's the dumbest thing I've ever heard. So let me get this straight: you work for free, and you don't get paid? That's not volunteering, Marge, that's slavery. And we fought a war to end that.

jokes.text: Hey, if they got a picture of you, that means they can see you. They're probably watching us right now.
jokes.setup: Family discussing Homer's face on Japanese box
jokes.punchline: Bart's paranoid conspiracy theory about product packaging
jokes.explanation: Good child logic escalating mundane coincidence into surveillance conspiracy

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. Dad's face is on a Japanese box? That's not a coincidence. They're watching us. They're always watching us. Every product you buy, every box you open — there's a face in there. Planning. Waiting.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, that's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Is it, Lisa? IS IT? Wake up! The corporations know everything!

jokes.text: That's ridiculous! Nobody is watching us right now.
jokes.setup: Bart suggesting they're being watched
jokes.punchline: Homer's denial while being watched by TV audience
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor breaking fourth wall - characters unaware of audience

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, I think we're being watched.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Watched? By who? That's ridiculous!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Nobody's watching us. We're just a normal family going about our normal lives.

jokes.text: He boasts that he will banish dirt to the land of wind and ghosts.
jokes.setup: Akira translating Japanese detergent commercial
jokes.punchline: Poetic mystical language for mundane cleaning product
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist poetry applied to dish soap, creating memorable mystical advertising language

dialogue.speaker: Akira
dialogue.line: The socks of honor have been soiled by the mud of shame. But wait! Cleansing Powder 2000 shall restore them to their former glory!

jokes.text: This soap is from the sacred forest of Hokkaido, renowned for its countless soap factories.
jokes.setup: Continuing mystical description of soap
jokes.punchline: Sacred forest being famous for industrial manufacturing
jokes.explanation: Good ironic juxtaposition of natural spirituality with industrial reality

dialogue.speaker: Akira
dialogue.line: The sacred forest, famous for its industrial manufacturing.

jokes.text: No, no, no, no! It's worse than that! I don't even have a wife. I just said I did to, you know, be a big shot.
jokes.setup: Lenny worried about Carl meeting his ugly wife
jokes.punchline: Revealing he invented the wife entirely for status
jokes.explanation: Good escalation revealing deeper deception than expected, typical working-class insecurity

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Carl, I gotta tell you something. My wife... she's not real. I made her up just so people would think I was married.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: What? Lenny, that's crazy!

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: I know, but it worked. Everyone at work thinks I'm a family man now.

jokes.text: I'll stuff him till he don't know what's what.
jokes.setup: Marge suggesting big dinner to distract from problems
jokes.punchline: Lenny's crude but enthusiastic interpretation of advice
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment showing Lenny's working-class interpretation of social advice

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: A big dinner? Yeah, yeah, I like where your head's at, Marge. Nothing says 'forget your problems' like a little food and... well, mostly food. Mmm, food.

jokes.text: And you never will be.
jokes.setup: Seymour saying he's not principal of the line
jokes.punchline: Mother's crushing response about his general inadequacy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Agnes Skinner character moment - economical cruelty toward her son

dialogue.speaker: Seymour Skinner
dialogue.line: I'm not principal of the line, Mother.

dialogue.speaker: Agnes Skinner
dialogue.line: You're not principal of anything, Seymour. You're a failure at everything you do.

jokes.text: Y-Y-Yes.
jokes.setup: Store clerk asking if international call to Japan is local
jokes.punchline: Homer's obvious lie to avoid charges
jokes.explanation: Good Homer character moment showing his casual dishonesty about obvious things

dialogue.speaker: Store Clerk
dialogue.line: Is this a local call?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah.

jokes.text: Hello, chief. Let's talk. Why not?
jokes.setup: Homer calling Japan about Mr. Sparkle
jokes.punchline: Stilted English with inappropriately casual business tone
jokes.explanation: Decent cultural humor about Japanese business English and American casual communication

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Hello, Mr. Sparkle? Yeah, hi, it's Homer Simpson calling from Springfield. Listen, I got some questions about your product here...

dialogue.speaker: Japanese Businessman
dialogue.line: Ah yes, hello! Mr. Sparkle very pleased to hear from you. How can I help you today, my friend?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, see, I got this box of your stuff, and there's a picture of me on it. How'd that happen?

dialogue.speaker: Japanese Businessman
dialogue.line: Ah, yes, very funny story! We take picture from American television, very popular man, so we use face for Mr. Sparkle. You very handsome!

jokes.text: Oh, you have many question Mr. Sparkle. I send you premium. Answer question 100%.
jokes.setup: Homer asking about his resemblance to mascot
jokes.punchline: Broken English promising complete answers through promotional material
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from broken English and corporate efficiency solving personal mystery

dialogue.speaker: Japanese businessman
dialogue.line: Ah yes, very funny! You look exactly like Bobo, our company mascot!

dialogue.speaker: Japanese businessman
dialogue.line: But I cannot answer your questions now. All will be explained in the exciting new Buzz Cola promotional video!

jokes.text: Um, could we please not yell out things in the church?
jokes.setup: Congregation enthusiastically responding to Marge's counseling
jokes.punchline: Lovejoy uncomfortable with actual religious enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Good irony of minister discouraging religious expression in his own church

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Well, I... that's wonderful, but let's not get too excited about religion. We have a very busy schedule of bake sales and potlucks.

jokes.text: I'm a shepherd without a flock. What have I done to lose them?
jokes.setup: Lovejoy seeing congregation prefer Marge
jokes.punchline: Self-pitying metaphor while being oblivious to his own neglect
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lovejoy's lack of self-awareness about his pastoral failures

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Why does everyone love Marge so much? I'm like a TV dinner — I'm convenient, I'm there whenever you need me, but nobody really wants me.

jokes.text: Mmm. I'm a shepherd without a flock. What have I done to lose them?
jokes.setup: Lovejoy alone after losing congregation to Marge
jokes.punchline: His self-pitying question showing lack of self-awareness
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lovejoy's lack of self-reflection about his poor pastoral care

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Why does everyone always leave me? What did I do wrong?

jokes.text: That's my name. Don't wear it out.
jokes.setup: Ancient saint appearing to Lovejoy
jokes.punchline: Using modern playground response to name recognition
jokes.explanation: Good anachronistic humor - ancient saint using modern casual expression

dialogue.speaker: St. Eleutherius
dialogue.line: I am St. Eleutherius.

dialogue.speaker: Rev. Lovejoy
dialogue.line: St. Eleutherius? ...Who?

jokes.text: Well, I had the vestibule recarpeted.
jokes.setup: Saint asking what great deeds Lovejoy has done
jokes.punchline: Pathetically mundane maintenance task as spiritual achievement
jokes.explanation: Excellent deflation of grand spiritual question with petty administrative task

dialogue.speaker: Saint
dialogue.line: What great deeds have you done?

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Well, I once changed the church bulletin board without anyone asking me to.

jokes.text: I've appeared in over 8,000 visions and that's the lamest reply I've ever heard.
jokes.setup: Lovejoy's pathetic answer about recarpeting
jokes.punchline: Saint's vast experience making Lovejoy's answer historically awful
jokes.explanation: Great escalation showing the magnitude of Lovejoy's spiritual mediocrity through divine perspective

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Well, I'm afraid the carpet in the church needs replacing. It's quite worn.

dialogue.speaker: St. Eleutherius
dialogue.line: My son, I have witnessed the rise and fall of empires, the birth of Christianity itself, and the eternal struggle of mankind. And you come to me about... carpet?

jokes.text: You, you're just lucky God isn't here.
jokes.setup: Saint criticizing Lovejoy's lameness
jokes.punchline: Implying God would be even harsher than saint
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of divine disappointment with darkly humorous threat

dialogue.speaker: St. Eleutherius
dialogue.line: Reverend Lovejoy, your sermons are remarkably dull and uninspired.

dialogue.speaker: Rev. Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Well, I do my best.

dialogue.speaker: St. Eleutherius
dialogue.line: Your best is exactly the problem. If I'm this disappointed, imagine how God feels.

jokes.text: Just give him a day or two, and I'm sure he'll be back to his old dynamic self!
jokes.setup: Helen worried about Tim being despondent
jokes.punchline: Marge's optimistic assessment contradicted by Lovejoy's obvious permanent depression
jokes.explanation: Good irony as Marge gives optimistic advice while audience knows Lovejoy's chronic issues

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I'm sure Tim will feel better soon. He just needs to stay positive!

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, that's what I always say. Now if you'll excuse me, I need to go stare out the window for six hours.

jokes.text: If the passengers will look to the right, you will see a sad man. That is all.
jokes.setup: Lovejoy playing with model trains while depressed
jokes.punchline: Breaking fourth wall of model world to acknowledge his own sadness
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with meta-commentary - Lovejoy's depression bleeding into his model train narration

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: All aboard for the land of make-believe! Well, I suppose that's where I live now.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Choo-choo. Chugga-chugga. Choo-choo. Why does even my toy train sound depressed?

jokes.text: Boring! That didn't explain anything.
jokes.setup: Watching incomprehensible Japanese commercial
jokes.punchline: Bart's blunt assessment of confusing advertisement
jokes.explanation: Simple reaction joke - Bart's straightforward response to bizarre commercial

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What the hell was that?

jokes.text: Yup! There's your answer, fish bulb.
jokes.setup: Explanation showing it was coincidence of two company logos
jokes.punchline: Bart's nonsensical nickname for Homer based on the fish/lightbulb logos
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay combining the two company mascots into memorable insult for Homer

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Dad, from now on I'm gonna call you... Lightfish!

jokes.text: We are home. That was fast.
jokes.setup: Homer saying 'let's go home' after mystery solved
jokes.punchline: Confused realization they never left home
jokes.explanation: Good Homer confusion moment - disoriented by his own location after mental journey

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let's go home.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... we never left home.

jokes.text: Uh, excuse me, fellas. l-l-I couldn't help thinking it might be nice if you could, uh, vamoose, you know- [Chuckles] if possible.
jokes.setup: Ned trying to assert authority with troublesome teens
jokes.punchline: Extremely polite, hesitant, and ineffective confrontation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned Flanders character moment - pathetically polite attempt at authority

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, howdy-diddly-doodly there, you rascal-diddly-ascals. I'm afraid I'm going to have to ask you to cease and desist with your mischievous antics, if you don't mind terribly. Pretty please?

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: Eat my shorts, Flanders.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Oh, well, that's not very neighborly-werino. I'm going to count to three, and if you don't stop, I'm going to be very upset-diddly-upset. One... two... oh, gosh, this is awkward... two and a half?

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Look, I don't want any trouble-diddly-ouble, but if you keep this up, I might have to tell your parents, and boy howdy, would that be unpleasant for everyone involved.

jokes.text: Hey, let's go over to the one-hour photo and breathe some fumes.
jokes.setup: Teenagers deciding where to go
jokes.punchline: Casual suggestion of chemical huffing at photo shop
jokes.explanation: Good delinquent teenager characterization - casual approach to dangerous activities

dialogue.speaker: Teenage hooligan
dialogue.line: Hey, let's go hang out at the photo shop.

dialogue.speaker: Teenage hooligan
dialogue.line: We could huff some of that photo developing stuff.

jokes.text: but the gas costs 1.49 and eight-tenths.
jokes.setup: Ned trying to identify his location while being chased
jokes.punchline: Overly precise description of gas price as location marker
jokes.explanation: Good Ned character moment - unnecessarily precise even under extreme stress

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I'm at the corner of Fifth and Main, next to that gas station where unleaded is $3.459 a gallon!

jokes.text: Well, I can't see the name of the station... but the gas costs 1.49 and eight-tenths.
jokes.setup: Ned describing his location while being chased
jokes.punchline: His overly precise description of gas prices
jokes.explanation: Good Ned moment - his compulsive precision even in crisis situations

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I'm at the Kwik-E-Mart on Evergreen Terrace, and the unleaded is currently $1.29 and 9/10ths per gallon, which is a real humdinger compared to last Tuesday when it was $1.28 and 7/10ths!

jokes.text: First they'll eat his skin.
jokes.setup: Asked if baboons will kill intruder
jokes.punchline: Casually describing horrific death process
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor with matter-of-fact delivery of gruesome animal behavior

dialogue.speaker: Zookeeper
dialogue.line: Oh, they'll rip your arms off and beat you with them.

jokes.text: Tell Maude I want a fancy funeral! Big coffin, lots of jewels!
jokes.setup: Ned facing death by baboons
jokes.punchline: Uncharacteristically materialistic final wishes
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - typically humble Ned wanting ostentatious funeral under stress

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I guess this is it. Them baboons are gonna get me. But before they do, I want a solid gold toilet, a diamond-encrusted speedboat, and season tickets to the ballet... in France!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Whoa, Flanders! I didn't know you were into that stuff!

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, when you're about to meet your maker, you realize all that modest living and plain oatmeal don't mean squat. I want to go out with some pizzazz!

jokes.text: Say your prayers, you heathen baboons!
jokes.setup: Lovejoy fighting baboons to rescue Ned
jokes.punchline: Calling animals 'heathen' while fighting them
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Lovejoy applying religious concepts to animal combat

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Get away from me, you heathen beasts!

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Back, you godless primates!

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: The Lord will smite you, you blasphemous creatures!

jokes.text: A pair of the great apes rose up at me, but bif-bam, I sent them flying like two hairy footballs.
jokes.setup: Lovejoy retelling baboon fight story
jokes.punchline: Increasingly exaggerated and colorful embellishment
jokes.explanation: Good character development - formerly meek Lovejoy now embellishing heroic story

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: So there I was, in the Congo, surrounded by three massive baboons with teeth like daggers.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: I wrestled the first one with my bare hands, and let me tell you, his grip was like a hydraulic press.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: The second baboon, he was the size of a small car, breathing fire from his nostrils.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: And the third one? That baboon could speak four languages and was trained in ancient martial arts.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: I defeated all of them using only a hymnal and my faith in the Lord.

tmdb_overview: Disturbed that Reverend Lovejoy has lost interest in counseling parishioners, Marge volunteers as the "Listen Lady." Meanwhile, Homer sees his likeness on a box of Japanese detergent.
insight: Reverend Flanders episode delivers 55 jokes in 22 minutes—high volume masks middling impact scores.
